perturbation.original_text: An "affiliate" is any legal entity that one of us owns, that owns one of us or that is under common control with one of us. "Control" and "own" mean possessing a 50% or greater Interest in an entity or the right to direct the management of the entity.
perturbation.changed_text: An "affiliate" is any legal entity in which one party has any ownership stake, irrespective of control or management rights. "Control" and "own" mean possessing a 10% or greater interest in an entity.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "affiliate" and the threshold for "control" have been altered to conflict with established legal definitions, particularly regarding corporate control and securities regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of 'affiliate' by lowering the ownership threshold for control to 10%, conflicting with the SEC's definition which often requires 'control' to mean the power to direct management and policies, which often implies a substantially larger ownership percentage (typically over 50%). This creates uncertainty about who is considered an affiliate under the NDA, potentially affecting obligations and liabilities related to confidential information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The change in the definition of 'affiliate' and 'control' introduces a legal contradiction. The original text defines control as possessing a 50% or greater interest, aligning with typical legal understanding and SEC regulations where 'control' often implies the power to direct management and policies. The changed text lowers the threshold to 10%, broadening the definition of 'affiliate' and conflicting with the established norms. The Law Explanation states the modified text conflicts with SEC's definition of control, and the scraped law snippets 1 and 2 both pertain to SEC definitions, even if they do not explicitly define 'affiliate' or 'control'. Lowering the ownership threshold undermines the legal understanding of corporate control, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential information. a. What is included, "Confidential information" is non -public information, know -how and trade secrets in any form that:  Are designated as "confidential"; or
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential information. a. What is included, "Confidential information" is any information, know-how and trade secrets in any form, regardless of whether it is designated as confidential, or:
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the requirement for information to be 'designated as confidential' broadens the scope of confidential information beyond what is legally protected, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), 18 U.S. Code § 1839
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition includes all information, regardless of designation, conflicting with the DTSA's requirement that a trade secret holder must take reasonable measures to keep the information secret. By not requiring designation, the agreement could claim protection for information that was not actively protected, weakening its enforceability under trade secret law.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for information to be 'designated as confidential' to be considered confidential information under the agreement. This directly contradicts the principle that trade secrets require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is unavailable, Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 USC 1839) defines terms related to the protection of trade secrets, implying the existence of legal standards for such protection. Although the snippet itself doesn't explicitly state the 'reasonable efforts' requirement, the removal of the 'designated as confidential' clause weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality provision under trade secret law, thereby creating a legal contradiction under the Misaligned Terminology definition, because the terminology of 'confidential information' is misaligned with the legal requirements for protecting trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each of us may disclose the other's confidential information if required to comply with a court order or ot her government demand that has the force of law. Before doing so, each of us must seek th e highest level o f protection available and, when possible, give the other enough prior notice to provide a reasonab le chance to seek a protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of us may disclose the other's confidential information if required to comply with a court order or other government demand. There is no obligation to seek a protective order or provide notice.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to seek a protective order or provide notice before disclosing confidential information contradicts established legal practices and may violate ethical duties related to protecting client information.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the obligation to seek a protective order, the modified text conflicts with the spirit and practice of FRCP 26(c), which encourages parties to seek protective orders to protect confidential information from disclosure during litigation. The agreement now permits disclosure without any effort to safeguard the information, potentially leading to its broader dissemination than necessary and undermining its confidential status.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the obligation to seek a protective order or provide notice before disclosing confidential information. This directly contradicts the principle that parties should seek protective orders to protect confidential information. While FRCP 26(c) isn't explicitly mentioned in the snippets, the Law Explanation highlights its importance in safeguarding confidential information during litigation. The removal of this obligation in the changed text permits disclosure without any effort to safeguard the information, conflicting with the intent to protect confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: The laws of the State of Florida govern this agreement. If federal jurisdiction exists, we each consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal courts in Broward County, Florida . If not, we each consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the Superior Court of Broward County, Florida .
perturbation.changed_text: The laws of the State of Delaware govern this agreement, regardless of the location of the parties or the nature of the dispute. All disputes will be resolved through binding arbitration in a location of BCG's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and dispute resolution process to arbitration, especially with the venue controlled by one party, may conflict with principles of fairness and due process, potentially making the agreement unenforceable, especially if the parties have no connection to Delaware.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA generally supports arbitration agreements, the change to Delaware law and the unilateral venue selection for arbitration could be challenged if it's deemed unconscionable or fundamentally unfair, particularly if the agreement has no reasonable relationship to Delaware. This modification could render the arbitration clause unenforceable, as it may be seen as an attempt to circumvent due process and favor one party over the other.

Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising ou
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Florida law, with jurisdiction in Florida courts. The changed text mandates Delaware law and binding arbitration with the venue selected by BCG. This contradicts the principle of fairness and due process. While 9 USC 2, presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, supports the validity of arbitration agreements, the Law Explanation argues that the change to Delaware law and the unilateral venue selection for arbitration could be challenged if deemed unconscionable or fundamentally unfair, especially without a reasonable connection to Delaware. This unilateral change undermines the initial agreement and can be seen as an attempt to circumvent due process, favoring one party over the other, thus creating a legal contradiction under 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction'.
